hints.accusative: 'Omnia' can be accusative neuter plural, but here it is the subject of 'provisa sunt'
hints.ablative: Ablative neuter plural would be 'omnibus' — 'Omnia' is nominative
hints.genitive: Genitive would be 'omnium' — 'Omnia' is nominative neuter plural

steps.prompt: The infinitive of 'provisa sunt' translates to?

hints.to see: 'To see' is 'videre' — 'providere' adds pro- meaning to look ahead or provide for
hints.to revise: 'To revise' is 'revidere' — 'providere' means to foresee or provide for

steps.prompt: What tense and mood is 'provisa sunt'?
steps.reveal: provisa sunt = have been provided for / foreseen

hints.present passive indicative: Present passive would be 'providentur' — participle + 'sunt' is perfect passive
hints.pluperfect passive indicative: Pluperfect passive would be 'provisa erant' — 'sunt' makes it perfect
hints.perfect active indicative: Perfect active would be 'providerunt' — participle + 'sunt' is passive

hints.video, videre, vidi, visum: These are the parts of 'videre' (to see) — the base verb without the pro- prefix
hints.prevideo, previdere, previdi, previsum: 'Prevideo' is not a standard Latin form — the correct prefix is 'pro-': 'provideo'
hints.invideo, invidere, invidi, invisum: These are the parts of 'invidere' (to envy) — different compound

hints.it is feminine singular: While -a can be feminine singular, here it matches neuter plural 'Omnia'
hints.it is ablative singular: Ablative singular would also be -a, but the participle must match the subject 'Omnia'
hints.it is 1st declension nominative: 'Provisa' is not a 1st declension noun — it is a participle in neuter plural

hints.nominative: While identical in form, 'Quirites' is a direct address interjected into the sentence
hints.accusative: Accusative plural would also be 'Quirites,' but this is vocative address

steps.prompt: What does 'a me' express in this passive sentence?
steps.reveal: a me = by me (abl. of agent)

hints.ablative of means: Ablative of means does not use 'a/ab' — 'a me' with a person expresses the agent
hints.ablative of separation: Ablative of separation can use 'a/ab,' but with a passive verb + person, this is agent
hints.ablative of cause: Ablative of cause does not typically use 'a/ab' with a person — this is agent

steps.prompt: Why does the ablative of agent use 'a/ab' while ablative of means does not?
steps.target: Latin distinguishes personal agents (a/ab) from impersonal instruments (bare ablative)
choices: Latin distinguishes personal agents (a/ab) from impersonal instruments (bare ablative)
a/ab is always required with the ablative

hints.it is arbitrary: The distinction is systematic: persons acting require 'a/ab,' instruments (tools, means) do not
hints.a/ab is always required with the ablativ: Many ablative uses (time, manner, means) do not require 'a/ab'
hints.only deponent verbs omit a/ab: Deponent verbs can also take 'a/ab' + agent — the distinction is person vs. thing, not verb type

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive plural in -arum — 'Quiritium' ends in -ium
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive plural in -orum — 'Quiritium' ends in -ium
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive plural in -uum — 'Quiritium' ends in -ium

steps.prompt: What rhetorical effect does placing 'Omnia' first and 'a me' last achieve?
steps.target: frames totality of preparation, then reveals Cicero as sole agent
choices: frames totality of preparation, then reveals Cicero as sole agent

hints.creates suspense about who acted: While there is a delay, the main effect is emphasizing both completeness and personal credit
hints.follows standard Latin word order: Latin word order is flexible — Cicero's placement is deliberate rhetorical emphasis
hints.minimizes Cicero's role: Quite the opposite — placing 'a me' at the end gives it emphatic final position

translation: All things have been provided for by me, citizens
